other_observers: Nick Komar, Paul Opler, Connie and Al Kogler, Warren Reeser; first found and identified by Nick Komar
description: the bird was a medium sized gull, noticeably smaller than nearby Herring Gulls and slightly larger than nearby California Gulls. The overall shape was similar to, but less bulky than Herring Gull, with a more rounded head, slimmer overall shape that was accentuated by the slimmer neck and proportionally longer wingtips. The bill was relatively petite, with very little gonydeal angle. The color of the bill was a dull greenish-yellow color, noticeably different than the brighter yellow-orange bill of practically every other gull in the flock. There was a small red gonydeal post, which also appeard to be a bit paler than on nearby gulls. The legs were bright pink, on the intense side compared to Herring Gull. The orbital ring and gape were pinkish red. The irides were yellow, though perhaps with a very slight dusky color apparent at very close range.  The head and underparts of the bird were completely white. The mantle was at least a couple of shades paler than nearby Herring and Ring-billed Gulls. The bird had a fairly large white tertial crescent. The wingtips were almost completely white, with very faint pale gray markings visible only at close range. On the spread wing these markings appeared to be near the tips of the outer primaries only, with the largest marking on p8 or p9. The tail of the bird was completely white.Behaviors: The bird was found on the edge of a large flock of gulls loafing on the edge of Boyd Lake. It was seen both perched and swimming, and very briefly flying, for about an hour total. The bird apparently flew after we left, and could not be found in the area despite much searching.Call: none heardPlumage: adult alternate
photographer: Photographer: Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer, Andrew Spencer
